titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 7 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: In this letter to his wife Lillian, from Wilfrid Spender, confesses that he is currently relatively idle due to lack of work, but he is glad that the men are being given a rest, as they could be sent back to the front again at any time. He asks Lillian, if she is staying with the Carsons, to pass on news of the Ulstermen's astonishing courage to Sir Edward Carson (1854-1935). Spender suggests that Sir Roger Casement (1864-1916) will not be executed, and will be 'feted within the year'. He gives some news of mutual acquaintances, suggests that the bad weather may prove to be 'the deciding factor' in the war, and states that the greatest heroes may be the prisoners taken by the Germans. This letter is part of a series of correspondence from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender remained in contact with the Ulster Unionist leadership, and consistently opposed Home Rule and the six-county solution.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
7 July My pr I had a day of
comparative stillness & am
beginning to feel that I ought
to be employed on something
if necessary being attached
to another division to help. For our men I am glad
indeed of the rest especially
as we may move at very
short notice anytime. It is hard to give you
any news which does not
fringe on forbidden subjects
& I do not want to set
a bad example tho' I know
your discretion. If you are staying with
the Carson's try to cheer him
up with an account of how
the Division has behaved.
Not even he could have
expected such heroism from
our men. I wonder if you
gave him my message the
other day. I look upon it
as important that he should
know in view of his apparent
intimacy with L. G. now. Sredward will trust my
word in this, I know.
I see that the Sinn Fein
rebels are all being rein-
stated now and have little
doubt that Casement
will not be executed & will
be fÃªted in less than
a year. The 'loyal rebels'
are apparently not to be
amnestied & Hamel and myâ
self might find ourselves in
a very difficult position. Yesterday a GHQ car
was going by and pulled
up sharply when who
should pop out but Butler
now Major Gen. 'Gen' Staff.
He shook me warmly by
the hand and asked after our
men. It was a curious
meeting after these years.
I did not have time to ask
after Mrs. Butler. He spoke
confidently but gave no
news of importance. The weather is bad
but tho hard for our men
I hope that it may affect
the German harvest which
might - on the Govt willed it â
prove the deciding factor in the
war. Would you send the en-
closed to Mrs. Galfkin (if still
at Ardglass?). Please enquire
from Col J Grng as to the
news of his brother. CC.
I feel so sorry for Mrs CC
especially until she hears that
he is a prisoner. I withdraw my former
remarks about prisoners now.
The greatest hero might
well be a prisoner in German
hands now. One is rather bland & not even the 'news' in the
Papers can cheer one up,
but I am fit for walk
& shall be glad of it. No Letter from you today.
Hope my letters after the
day turned up all right. Your bw WB Spender
